note: Verified by the fact-checker against fetched pages. Beluga NOAA stakes + paper metadata high-confidence; acoustic model internals are abstract-deep only (full text paywalled). Population framing is HEDGED per NOAA: 331 may be stabilizing/possibly increasing, yet has not recovered as expected. Caribou set is the verified runner-up fallback.

beluga.text: A 2026 study titled "Adaptive Acoustic Monitoring for Endangered Cook Inlet Beluga Whales in Complex Soundscapes" was published in the journal Marine Mammal Science.
beluga.value: DOI 10.1111/mms.70213; Marine Mammal Science, vol. 42, issue 3, article e70213

beluga.text: The paper was published online first on June 7, 2026, with a print issue dated July 2026.

The study was a NOAA Fisheries, Microsoft AI for Good, and Alaska Department of Fish and Game collaboration; authors include Verena Gill (NOAA Fisheries, Anchorage), Manuel Castellote and Rahul Dodhia and Juan M. Lavista Ferres (Microsoft AI for Good Research Lab), and Lori Polasek (Alaska Department of Fish and Game).
beluga.value: 8 authors across Microsoft AI for Good, NOAA Fisheries, ADF&G

beluga.text: The framework is a deep-learning dual-stage model that separates signal detection from species classification.
The model expands beyond belugas to a multi-species context that also includes killer whales and humpback whales.

Using contrastive audio-language models and active learning, the framework improved detection of rare species occurrences and strengthened confidence in daily beluga presence estimates across Cook Inlet regions.
NOAA leads a separate collaborative effort with the U.S. Geological Survey, Microsoft AI for Good, the Naval Research Laboratory and other partners to detect Cook Inlet belugas in satellite imagery.
beluga.value: NOAA + USGS + Microsoft AI for Good + Naval Research Laboratory

beluga.text: The satellite initiative aims to build a secure cloud-based application that uses machine learning to detect whales and other marine mammals in very high-resolution (VHR) satellite imagery.
beluga.value: cloud app detecting whales in VHR satellite imagery

beluga.text: The best current abundance estimate for the Cook Inlet beluga population is 331 whales, suggesting the population may be stabilizing and possibly increasing.
The Cook Inlet beluga population declined by nearly 80 percent between 1979 and 2018, from about 1,300 whales to an estimated 279.
In 2008, the Cook Inlet beluga population was listed as endangered under the Endangered Species Act.
In 2011, NOAA Fisheries designated two areas of Cook Inlet as critical habitat.
NOAA Fisheries identifies five beluga whale stocks in Alaskan waters, and the Cook Inlet stock is geographically isolated from the others.
NOAA lists threats to Cook Inlet belugas including ocean noise, prey limitations, habitat impacts, contaminants, predation, strandings, disease and changing climate conditions.
beluga.value: ocean noise, prey limitations, habitat impacts, contaminants, predation, strandings, disease, climate

beluga.text: Although the subsistence hunt has been suspended since 2007, the Cook Inlet beluga population has not recovered as expected.
beluga.value: hunt suspended 2007; not recovered as expected

beluga.text: The Cook Inlet beluga whale is managed as a Distinct Population Segment (DPS).

caribou_fallback.text: A Frontiers in Ecology and Evolution study applied the HerdNet computer-vision model to aerial survey imagery covering the contiguous ranges of five Alaska caribou herds (Central Arctic, Fortymile, Porcupine, Teshekpuk, Western Arctic).
The best HerdNet variant reached an F1 of about 95.5 percent on full-image caribou detection.
On the 2019 test set HerdNet reached a Total Counting Error of about 3.1 percent.
In the OWL benchmark, OWL-C fine-tuned on the 2017 Porcupine Caribou Herd split attained F1 = 0.965.
OWL showed a signed count error of +3.1 percent on ADF&G's 2022 Central Arctic survey test patches.
Point-level annotations are reported up to seven times slower and three times more expensive to produce than bounding-box annotations.
